name: Naturepedia Ocean Systems Knowledge Mesh
description: Machine-readable semantic knowledge mesh connecting Earth Systems™, Ocean Systems™, Water Systems™, Weather™, Carbon Cycle™, coastal and hydrothermal ecosystems, marine food webs, biodiversity, wildlife, field locations, natural pattern formation, ocean observation, and stewardship.

hasPart.name: Earth Systems → Ocean Relationship
hasPart.description: The global ocean connects atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere, cryosphere, climate, water, carbon, and living systems.

hasPart.name: Atmosphere → Ocean Relationship
hasPart.description: Solar energy, winds, storms, precipitation, evaporation, heat, moisture, gases, and momentum regulate ocean-surface behavior.

hasPart.name: Water Systems → Ocean Relationship
hasPart.description: Groundwater, rivers, wetlands, floodplains, watersheds, and estuaries deliver freshwater, sediment, nutrients, carbon, and organic material to coastal and marine systems.

hasPart.name: Ocean Circulation Network
hasPart.description: Winds, planetary rotation, temperature, salinity, density, basin geometry, currents, gyres, eddies, fronts, upwelling, and downwelling form a connected transport system.

hasPart.name: Ocean Zone Connectivity
hasPart.description: Surface, twilight, deep, abyssal, hadal, coastal, open-ocean, and seafloor environments exchange energy, organisms, nutrients, oxygen, and carbon.

hasPart.name: Marine Nutrient and Food-Web Network
hasPart.description: Sunlight, nutrients, phytoplankton, microbes, zooplankton, fish, seabirds, marine mammals, predators, decomposers, and recycled materials form interconnected marine food webs.

hasPart.name: Ocean Carbon Network
hasPart.description: Atmosphere–ocean exchange, photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, particle sinking, deep-water transport, and sediment burial connect ocean systems with the global carbon cycle.

hasPart.name: Coastal Connectivity
hasPart.description: Watersheds, rivers, estuaries, wetlands, tides, intertidal zones, continental shelves, nearshore habitats, and open-ocean processes operate as connected transition systems.

hasPart.name: Hydrothermal Connectivity
hasPart.description: Seafloor fractures, geothermal heat, seawater circulation, mineral-rich discharge, microbes, chemosynthesis, and specialized organisms connect geology with deep-ocean ecosystems.

hasPart.name: Ocean Pattern Formation
hasPart.description: Energy, motion, rotation, density differences, gravity, boundaries, and feedback produce related patterns across ripples, waves, tides, fronts, eddies, gyres, coastlines, and planetary circulation.

hasPart.name: Ocean Observation Network
hasPart.description: Satellites, buoys, profiling floats, research vessels, underwater vehicles, wildlife monitoring, field locations, models, and human expertise create distributed ocean knowledge.

hasPart.name: Knowledge → Stewardship Relationship
hasPart.description: Observation, validation, modeling, interpretation, communication, conservation, and governance connect scientific understanding with ocean stewardship.
